results.possibleIdentity: Jean Dupuy (1925–2021) was a French-born American artist recognized as a pioneer in combining art and technology. Originally a lyrical abstractionist painter in Paris, he moved to New York in 1967, where he famously destroyed much of his earlier work to focus on technological and kinetic art. In 1968, he gained international recognition by winning a competition organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) for his sculpture 'Cone Pyramid' (also known as 'Heart Beats Dust'). This work used a stethoscope and amplifier to shoot red pigment into the air in rhythm with the viewer's heartbeat and was featured in the landmark exhibition 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). Throughout the late 1960s and early 1970s, he remained active in the New York avant-garde, teaching at the School of Visual Arts (1969–1971) and participating in the 'Art & Technology' exhibition at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art in 1971. In the mid-1970s, Dupuy shifted his focus toward performance art and Fluxus-related activities, organizing collaborative events such as 'Soup & Tart' (1974) and 'Three Evenings on a Revolving Stage' (1976). His later career was characterized by a move to Nice, France, where he specialized in anagrammatic works and artist books.

segment.text: In 1968, he gained international recognition by winning a competition organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) for his sculpture 'Cone Pyramid' (also known as 'Heart Beats Dust').

This work used a stethoscope and amplifier to shoot red pigment into the air in rhythm with the viewer's heartbeat and was featured in the landmark exhibition 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA).

Throughout the late 1960s and early 1970s, he remained active in the New York avant-garde, teaching at the School of Visual Arts (1969–1971) and participating in the 'Art & Technology' exhibition at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art in 1971.

In the mid-1970s, Dupuy shifted his focus toward performance art and Fluxus-related activities, organizing collaborative events such as 'Soup & Tart' (1974) and 'Three Evenings on a Revolving Stage' (1976).
